hints.genitive: Genitive would be 'Caesaris' — 'Caesar' is nominative, the subject
hints.accusative: Accusative would be 'Caesarem' — 'Caesar' is nominative here
hints.vocative: Caesar is not being addressed — he is the subject of 'maturat'

hints.1st declension: 1st declension has genitive in -ae — 'Caesaris' ends in -is
hints.2nd declension: 2nd declension has genitive in -i — 'Caesaris' ends in -is
hints.4th declension: 4th declension has genitive in -ūs — 'Caesaris' ends in -is

hints.a preposition meaning 'with': 'Cum' with a noun means 'with', but here it introduces a clause with subjunctive 'esset'
hints.a result clause: Result clauses use 'ut' + subjunctive — 'cum' + subjunctive is temporal/causal
hints.a purpose clause: Purpose clauses use 'ut' + subjunctive — 'cum' + subjunctive is temporal/causal

hints.supine: The supine looks the same but appears with verbs of motion — here 'nuntiatum' is part of a passive construction with 'esset'
hints.present active participle: Present active participle would be 'nuntians' — 'nuntiatum' with -tum is perfect passive
hints.gerundive: Gerundive would be 'nuntiandum' — 'nuntiatum' is the perfect passive participle

hints.to eat: That would be 'edere' — 'esset' comes from 'esse' (to be)
hints.to go out: That would be 'exire' — 'esset' comes from 'esse' (to be)
hints.to stand: That would be 'stare' — 'esset' comes from 'esse' (to be)

hints.present subjunctive: Present subjunctive would be 'sit' — 'esset' with the -sse- stem is imperfect subjunctive
hints.perfect subjunctive: Perfect subjunctive would be 'fuerit' — 'esset' is imperfect subjunctive
hints.imperfect indicative: Imperfect indicative would be 'erat' — 'esset' with -et ending is subjunctive

hints.edo, esse, edi, esum: These are the parts of 'edere/esse' (to eat) — 'esset' here is from 'esse' (to be)
hints.eo, ire, ii, itum: These are the parts of 'ire' (to go)
hints.sto, stare, steti, statum: These are the parts of 'stare' (to stand)

hints.to mature: While related to 'maturus', 'maturare' in Caesar means 'to hasten/hurry'
hints.to measure: That would be 'metiri' — 'maturat' comes from 'maturare' (to hasten)
hints.to remain: That would be 'manere' — 'maturat' comes from 'maturare' (to hasten)

hints.perfect indicative: Perfect would be 'maturavit' — 'maturat' is present indicative
hints.present subjunctive: Present subjunctive would be 'maturet' — 'maturat' with -at is present indicative (1st conj.)
hints.future indicative: Future would be 'maturabit' — 'maturat' is present indicative

hints.moror, morari, moratus sum: These are the parts of 'morari' (to delay)
hints.metior, metiri, mensus sum: These are the parts of 'metiri' (to measure)
hints.maneo, manere, mansi, mansum: These are the parts of 'manere' (to remain)

hints.nominative: Nominative would be 'urbs' — 'urbe' with -e is ablative after 'ab'
hints.dative: Dative would be 'urbi' — 'urbe' is ablative, governed by 'ab'
hints.accusative: Accusative would be 'urbem' — 'urbe' is ablative after 'ab'

hints.1st declension: 1st declension has genitive in -ae — 'urbis' ends in -is
hints.2nd declension: 2nd declension has genitive in -i — 'urbis' ends in -is
hints.5th declension: 5th declension has genitive in -ei — 'urbis' ends in -is

hints.to profit: That would be 'prodesse' — 'proficisci' is a deponent meaning 'to set out/depart'
hints.to advance: That would be 'progredi' — 'proficisci' means 'to set out/depart'
hints.to flee: That would be 'fugere' — 'proficisci' means 'to set out/depart'

hints.perfect infinitive: Perfect infinitive would be 'profectum esse' — 'proficisci' is present infinitive
hints.present indicative: 'Proficisci' ends in -i, the deponent infinitive ending — it complements 'maturat'
hints.imperfect subjunctive: Imperfect subjunctive would be 'proficisceretur' — 'proficisci' is present infinitive

hints.proficio, proficere, profeci, profectum: These are the parts of 'proficere' (to accomplish) — 'proficisci' is a deponent verb
hints.fugio, fugere, fugi, fugitum: These are the parts of 'fugere' (to flee)
hints.progredior, progredi, progressus sum: These are the parts of 'progredi' (to advance) — similar but different deponent

translation: When this had been reported to Caesar, he hastens to set out from the city
